title: UConn vs UCLA — Round of 32 Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • Round of 32 • Philadelphia, PA
venue: Xfinity Mobile Arena, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania • TNT
extraInfo: Winner faces #3 St. John’s or #6 Nebraska in the Sweet 16

stakes.callout: Two of the most storied programs in college basketball history meet in a Round of 32 showdown. 17 combined national championships — UConn's 6, UCLA's 11 — the most ever in a single NCAA Tournament game. UConn is chasing a third title in four years. UCLA carries John Wooden's legacy into every March. Both teams are banged up: UConn may be without its starting point guard, UCLA may be without its leading scorer. This is blue blood basketball at its finest.

cards.label: For UConn
cards.value: Alex Karaban is playing in his final NCAA Tournament — two national titles, 119+ career wins, the most games started in UConn history. Tarris Reed Jr. just posted 31 points and 27 rebounds against Furman — a performance not seen since Elvin Hayes in 1968. Dan Hurley's teams have won conference tournament games then lost before — and still cut down the nets in April. The 2023 championship team lost its Big East Tournament too.

cards.label: For UCLA
cards.value: UCLA beat UCF without its best player — Tyler Bilodeau sat with a knee sprain and the Bruins still found a way. Eric Dailey Jr. scored 20. Skyy Clark lost a tooth diving for a loose ball. If Bilodeau returns, UCLA gets back a 6-9 forward shooting 46.4% from three — the kind of weapon that changes a game's math entirely.

cards.label: 17 Championships
cards.value: UConn has 6 men's basketball titles (1999, 2004, 2011, 2014, 2023, 2024). UCLA has 11 (10 under John Wooden, 1 under Jim Harrick in 1995). That's 17 combined — more than any other possible NCAA Tournament matchup. The last time these teams met in the tournament was the 1995 Elite Eight, when UCLA won 102-96 on its way to the national title.

cards.label: Injury Watch
cards.value: UConn starting point guard Silas Demary Jr. (high ankle sprain) missed the Furman game and is questionable. Forward Jaylin Stewart (knee) has been out since late February. UCLA's Tyler Bilodeau (knee sprain from Big Ten Tournament) missed the UCF game and is a game-time decision. Both teams may be playing short-handed — which makes depth and coaching even more important.

styles.title: Championship Defense, Reed Dominance
bullets: Defense defines the identity: UConn allows just 65.3 PPG (11th nationally) with a KenPom adjusted defensive efficiency around 98.2 (23rd). Physical, pack-line influenced defense with elite rim protection. Dan Hurley's calling card is making opponents uncomfortable every possession.

Tarris Reed Jr. is a force of nature: The 6-10, 260-pound center averages 14.3 PPG and 8.7 RPG on 63.5% shooting. Against Furman, he went for 31 and 27 — the most dominant NCAA Tournament stat line in nearly 60 years. He creates gravity that opens everything else.

Five-headed scoring attack: Reed (14.3), Ball (13.4), Karaban (12.9), Mullins (12.0), Demary (10.9). No single player dominates, so you can't game-plan for one guy. The scoring leader shifts from game to game.

Three-point shooting is streaky: UConn shot 5-of-25 from three against Furman (20%). Karaban (38.9%) and Demary (41.6%) are reliable, but the team can go ice cold. If shots don't fall from deep, they lean even harder on Reed inside.

Injuries create uncertainty: Without Demary (6.2 APG, 41.6% from three), UConn loses its primary playmaker and best floor spacer. Without Stewart, the rotation shortens. The Huskies beat Furman without both — but UCLA is a much bigger challenge.

styles.title: Elite Shooting, Next-Man-Up Grit
bullets: Three 40%+ three-point shooters: Bilodeau (46.4%), Clark (43.2%), and Perry (41.2%) give UCLA the most dangerous perimeter trio in the tournament. Even without Bilodeau, Clark and Perry shot a combined 4-for-11 from deep against UCF. With Bilodeau back, the floor spacing becomes elite.

Dent distributes at an elite level: Donovan Dent averages 7.6 APG (4th nationally) with a 4.03 assist-to-turnover ratio (5th nationally). He recorded a triple-double in the Big Ten Tournament — the first in tournament history. He finds shooters and creates easy looks.

Defense showed up against UCF: UCLA held UCF to 37.5% shooting despite the Bruins' season-long defensive struggles (179th in KenPom). This is a team capable of locking in defensively when it matters most — a Cronin trademark from his Cincinnati days.

Interior questions: Xavier Booker (6-11, 15 pts vs. UCF) and Dailey (6-8) provide size, but neither matches UConn's interior bulk. Reed (6-10, 260) and Reibe (7-1, 260) present a physical challenge UCLA hasn't faced all season.

The Bilodeau variable: If he plays, UCLA gets back a player averaging 17.6 PPG on 46.4% from three at 6-9 — the most efficient scorer in the Big Ten. If he sits, UCLA needs Dailey and Booker to replicate their UCF heroics against a far better defense.

keys.title: The Bilodeau question changes everything.
keys.description: If Tyler Bilodeau plays, UCLA has a legitimate 6-9 shooter knocking down threes at 46.4%. That stretches UConn's defense in ways few teams can. If he sits, UCLA goes from a dangerous 7-seed to a team that barely survived UCF. Bilodeau's game-time decision is the single biggest variable in this matchup. UCLA won without him once. Doing it against UConn's defense is a different proposition entirely.

keys.title: Can anyone slow down Tarris Reed Jr.?
keys.description: Reed just posted 31 points and 27 rebounds on 80% shooting against Furman. UCLA's biggest body is Xavier Booker at 6-11, 240 — giving up 20+ pounds to Reed. Booker had 15 and 8 against UCF, so he can compete. But Reed is playing at a historic level right now. If UCLA can't limit his touches in the post and keep him off the offensive glass (he had 11 offensive boards vs. Furman), UConn will grind them down inside.

keys.title: UConn's three-point shooting must improve.
keys.description: UConn went 5-for-25 from three against Furman (20%). That won't work against UCLA. The Bruins will pack the paint to slow Reed and dare UConn's perimeter players to beat them. If Karaban (38.9%) and the guards can't hit open threes, UConn's offense becomes one-dimensional. The Huskies need balanced scoring — not just Reed heroics — to pull away.

keys.title: Demary's absence vs. Dent's distribution.
keys.description: If Silas Demary Jr. (6.2 APG, 41.6% from three) can't go, UConn loses its best playmaker and floor spacer. Donovan Dent (7.6 APG) is the best distributor in the game either way. UCLA's advantage in point guard play could be decisive — Dent creates easy shots for shooters, while UConn without Demary relies more on iso scoring and Reed post-ups. The team that generates better looks wins.

keys.title: Tournament DNA matters — but so does health.
keys.description: UConn has won six national championships. Karaban has played in two title games. Hurley has coached in four Final Fours' worth of tournament games. That experience is real. But UCLA beat UCF with a broken-tooth-diving-for-a-loose-ball mentality that screams tournament toughness. Cronin took UCLA to the Final Four as an 11-seed in 2021. Neither program flinches in March. This comes down to which team is healthier and makes shots.

prediction.summary: This is a heavyweight matchup between two banged-up blue bloods. UConn's interior dominance — Tarris Reed just went for 31 and 27 — is the most reliable weapon on either side. UCLA's perimeter shooting is the great equalizer, but only if Bilodeau plays. Without him, UCLA is asking role players to outshoot a championship-caliber defense. With him, this is a coin flip. The projection assumes Bilodeau plays but is limited, and Demary remains out for UConn. UConn's defense, Reed's dominance, and Karaban's experience should be enough to survive — but UCLA will make it a fight.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: UConn comes out physical, feeding Reed in the post early. He converts two quick buckets over Booker. But UCLA's shooters are dialed in — Perry hits an early three and Dent finds Clark for another. Bilodeau, if playing, tests his knee with a mid-range jumper. The game is tight, 16-14 UConn, with both defenses setting the tone.

phases.title: Mid-First Half to Halftime
phases.description: Karaban steadies UConn with two threes — he shoots 38.9% from deep and this is his final March. UConn's defense tightens, forcing UCLA into contested shots. But Dent (7.6 APG) keeps finding cutters and the Bruins stay within striking distance. Reed dominates the glass, grabbing four offensive rebounds in the half. UConn leads 34-28 at the break — a low-scoring grind typical of Hurley and Cronin teams.

phases.title: Second Half Push
phases.description: UCLA makes its run. Bilodeau hits a three that silences the UConn-heavy Philadelphia crowd. Clark drills another. Suddenly it's a two-point game. Hurley calls a fiery timeout. UConn responds through Reed — a put-back dunk and a jump hook — but UCLA's shooting keeps the pressure on. The lead seesaws between 2 and 7 points for the next eight minutes.

phases.title: Final 5 Minutes
phases.description: Reed's free throw shooting (56.1%) becomes a factor — he splits a pair at the line when UConn could have extended the lead. Dent's 63.6% FT shooting haunts UCLA too, as he misses the front end of a one-and-one. But Karaban — the two-time champion playing his final game if UConn loses — hits a pull-up jumper with two minutes left to push the lead to 5. UCLA gets one more look, but Clark's three rims out. UConn survives.

projectedScore.rationale: UConn's interior dominance — led by Reed's historic tournament form — outweighs UCLA's perimeter shooting, especially if Bilodeau is limited. Karaban's championship experience and Hurley's defensive scheme carry the Huskies in a low-scoring grind.

players.name: Tarris Reed Jr.
players.meta: F/C • 6-10, 260 lbs • Senior • vs. Furman: 31 pts, 27 reb
players.bio:
From St. Louis, Missouri. Transferred from Michigan after Juwan Howard was fired. Won Big East Sixth Man of the Year in 2024-25, then became a dominant starter this season. Against Furman, posted 31 points on 12-of-15 shooting and 27 rebounds (11 offensive) — the first player since Elvin Hayes in 1968 with 30+ points and 25+ rebounds in a tournament game. At 6-10, 260 pounds, he creates gravity that opens up everything. The catch: .561 free throw shooting makes him hackable late in games.
Read more →

players.name: Alex Karaban
players.meta: F • 6-8 • R-Senior • 2x National Champion, All-Big East First Team
players.bio:
From Northboro, Massachusetts. UConn's all-time winningest player (119+ wins) and all-time leader in games started (136+). Two-time national champion (2023, 2024). First active men's player inducted into the Huskies of Honor. Scored 22 points vs. Furman to complement Reed's monster night. This is his final March — a fifth-year senior whose career spans from the championship dynasty to the reload. He's the last link to back-to-back titles.
Read more →

players.name: Solo Ball
players.meta: G • 6-3 • Junior • Jerry West Award Finalist, 1,000-Point Scorer
players.bio:
From Leesburg, Virginia. Attended Brewster Academy. Originally committed to Richmond before flipping to UConn. Won a national championship as a freshman in 2024. Named a Top Five Finalist for the Jerry West Shooting Guard of the Year. Became the 54th player in UConn history to score 1,000 career points. Scored a career-high 26 points against Butler in the Big East opener. A third-year player who's developed from a rotation piece to a go-to scorer.
Read more →

players.name: Braylon Mullins
players.meta: G • 6-6 • Freshman • Indiana Mr. Basketball, McDonald's All-American
players.bio:
From Greenfield, Indiana. All-time leading scorer at Greenfield-Central HS with 2,158 points (set the school record with 52 in a single game). Named 2025 Indiana Mr. Basketball and McDonald's All-American. Preseason Big East Freshman of the Year. Missed 7 games early with ankle and head injuries but emerged as a dynamic scorer upon return. At 6-6, he's an oversized guard with NBA-level instincts — a projected lottery pick who could explode in March.
Read more →

players.name: Tyler Bilodeau
players.meta: F • 6-9 • Senior • All-Big Ten Third Team • Game-Time Decision (knee)
players.bio:
From Kennewick, Washington. Three-star recruit who developed at Oregon State (14.3 PPG as a sophomore) before transferring to UCLA. Shoots 46.4% from three at 6-9 — one of the most impossible-to-guard stat lines in the tournament. Hit the game-winning three to upset #4 Purdue in January. Scored a career-high 34 against UC Riverside. Named All-Big Ten Third Team. Missed the UCF game with a knee sprain from the Big Ten Tournament — his status is the biggest variable in this game.
Read more →

players.name: Donovan Dent
players.meta: G • 6-2 • Senior • 4th nationally in APG, California Mr. Basketball
players.bio:
From Riverside, California. Named California Mr. Basketball and Wooden High School Player of the Year at Centennial HS, where he led his team to a 33-1 record and a state title. Starred at New Mexico for three years — Mountain West Player of the Year as a junior (20.4 PPG, 6.4 APG). Chose UCLA over Kentucky. Recorded the first triple-double in Big Ten Tournament history (12-10-12 vs. Rutgers). The best pure distributor in this game — his 4.03 assist-to-turnover ratio is 5th nationally.
Read more →

players.name: Eric Dailey Jr.
players.meta: F • 6-8, 230 lbs • Junior • vs. UCF: 20 pts
players.bio:
From Bradenton, Florida (IMG Academy). His mother Shell was a national champion with the Texas women's basketball program; his father Eric Sr. played professionally. Transferred from Oklahoma State. Scored 20 points to lead UCLA past UCF when Bilodeau was out — the quintessential next-man-up performance. At 6-8, 230, he provides the size and physicality UCLA needs against UConn's bigs.
Read more →

players.name: Trent Perry
players.meta: G • 6-4 • Sophomore • McDonald's All-American, California Mr. Basketball
players.bio:
From West Covina, California (Harvard-Westlake School). Named a McDonald's All-American and California Mr. Basketball as a senior. Originally committed to USC but decommitted when Andy Enfield left for SMU. Went from averaging 3.7 PPG as a freshman to 12.8 PPG as a sophomore — one of the biggest leaps in the Big Ten. Scored a career-high 30 points against Penn State (22 in the second half). Shoots 41.2% from three — part of UCLA's deadly perimeter trio.
Read more →

funFacts.title: 17 Combined National Championships
funFacts.body: UConn has 6 men's basketball titles. UCLA has 11. That's 17 combined — more than any possible NCAA Tournament matchup between two schools. Add UConn's 11 women's titles and the university alone has 17.

funFacts.title: Reed's Historic Night
funFacts.body: Tarris Reed Jr. posted 31 points and 27 rebounds against Furman — the first player since Elvin Hayes in 1968 to record 30+ points and 25+ rebounds in an NCAA Tournament game. He shot 12-for-15 from the floor (80%).

funFacts.title: The Tooth Fairy Game (UCLA)
funFacts.body: Skyy Clark had a tooth knocked out diving for a loose ball against UCF. Walk-on Jack Seidler found the tooth on the court. Clark then hit the clinching free throw. That's March Madness, baby.

funFacts.title: Jonathan Goes to Antarctica (UConn)
funFacts.body: UConn's live mascot is a Siberian Husky named Jonathan (currently Jonathan XV). One of the early Jonathans traveled to Antarctica with Admiral Byrd's Operation Highjump in 1946-47.

funFacts.title: 46.4% From Three at 6-9 (UCLA)
funFacts.body: Tyler Bilodeau shoots 46.4% from three at 6-9. He hit the game-winning three to upset #4 Purdue in January. If he plays, he's the most efficient perimeter shooter on the court by a wide margin.

funFacts.title: He Turned Down the Lakers (UConn)
funFacts.body: After winning back-to-back titles, Dan Hurley turned down a massive offer from the Los Angeles Lakers in June 2024. LeBron James publicly praised him: "He's so DAMN GOOD!! Along with his staff."

funFacts.title: Triple-Double in Tournament History (UCLA)
funFacts.body: Donovan Dent recorded 12 points, 10 rebounds, and 12 assists against Rutgers in the Big Ten Tournament — the first triple-double in Big Ten Tournament history.

funFacts.title: The Winningest Husky (UConn)
funFacts.body: Alex Karaban has 119+ career wins — the most in UConn men's basketball history. He's played in two national championship games and won both. This is his final March.

funFacts.title: The Wooden Standard (UCLA)
funFacts.body: John Wooden won 10 national championships at UCLA in 12 years (1964-1975), including 7 consecutive titles (1967-73) and an 88-game winning streak. His players include Kareem Abdul-Jabbar and Bill Walton.

funFacts.title: The Hurley Basketball Family (UConn)
funFacts.body: Dan Hurley's father Bob Hurley Sr. won over 1,000 games as a high school coach in Jersey City. His brother Bobby Hurley was a Duke star and now coaches Arizona State. Basketball is the family business.

funFacts.title: From 3.7 to 12.8 PPG (UCLA)
funFacts.body: Trent Perry averaged 3.7 PPG as a freshman and 12.8 PPG as a sophomore. He originally committed to USC, then decommitted when the coach left for SMU. His breakout has been UCLA's biggest surprise.

funFacts.title: 1995 Elite Eight: The Last Meeting
funFacts.body: The last time UConn and UCLA met in the NCAA Tournament was March 25, 1995. UCLA won 102-96 in the Elite Eight behind Toby Bailey (26 pts) and Tyus Edney (22 pts, 10 ast). UCLA went on to win the national championship — their 11th and most recent title.

history.callout: This is the most championship-laden Round of 32 matchup imaginable. UConn's 6 titles (1999, 2004, 2011, 2014, 2023, 2024) plus UCLA's 11 titles (1964, 1965, 1967-73, 1975, 1995) equal 17 national championships. Their only previous NCAA Tournament meeting was a classic: UCLA 102, UConn 96 in the 1995 Elite Eight, with UCLA going on to win it all. Thirty-one years later, they meet again.

timeline.text: Rip Hamilton leads UConn past Duke 77-74 for the program's first national championship. UConn was a 9.5-point underdog.

Emeka Okafor powers UConn to its second title, beating Georgia Tech 82-73. The same year, the UConn women won their third title — the only school to sweep men's and women's championships in the same year.
Kemba Walker wins 5 games in 5 days at the Big East Tournament, then cuts down the nets nationally. UConn beats Butler 53-41 for title No. 3.
Shabazz Napier carries UConn to the title as a 7-seed, beating Kentucky 60-54. Three years after Kemba, another magical March run.
Dan Hurley's back-to-back championships. The 2024 team won every tournament game by double digits — average margin of 23.3 points. Hurley then turned down the Lakers.
John Wooden wins 10 national championships in 12 years, including 7 consecutive from 1967-73. Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, Bill Walton, and 88 straight wins. The greatest dynasty in college sports history.

UCLA beats UConn 102-96 in the Elite Eight, then wins the national championship under Jim Harrick. It remains UCLA's most recent title — and the only prior meeting between these two programs in the tournament.
Mick Cronin takes UCLA to the Final Four as an 11-seed. They lose to Gonzaga on Jalen Suggs' half-court buzzer-beater in overtime — one of the most iconic shots in tournament history.

subsections.title: 1995: The Only Prior Meeting
subsections.content:
On March 25, 1995, UCLA and UConn met in the Elite Eight at the Oakland Coliseum Arena. It was a shootout — UCLA 102, UConn 96. Toby Bailey scored 26 points. Tyus Edney had 22 points and 10 assists. Ray Allen scored 36 for UConn in a losing effort.

UCLA went on to beat Oklahoma State in the semifinals and Arkansas in the final to win their 11th national championship — the first since John Wooden's last title in 1975. Now, 31 years later, the programs meet again in the same tournament. The stakes are lower (Round of 32 vs. Elite Eight), but the history is unmistakable. Two programs that have combined for 17 titles, meeting in March once more.
